content.text: Structured output validation is the practice of enforcing a typed contract on every response an LLM produces before that response enters your application's business logic. In Python agentic pipelines, Pydantic BaseModel is the dominant mechanism for this: a class definition simultaneously acts as a JSON Schema for the LLM prompt, a runtime validator for the response, and a typed Python object for downstream code. Validating at every agent boundary prevents silent data corruption, enables self-healing retry loops, and makes pipelines debuggable at scale.
Pydantic is a Python library that enforces data types and constraints using standard Python type annotations. A
is a class that inherits from
and declares each expected field as a typed class attribute. When you call

with a dictionary, Pydantic checks every value against the declared type, runs registered field validators, enforces constraints (minimum length, numeric range, regex pattern), and either returns a fully typed Python object or raises a structured
that precisely identifies every field that failed.

In agentic AI, Pydantic models are the primary boundary between the LLM's untyped string world and the rest of your application's strongly typed world. LLMs are prompted to return JSON, but that JSON is embedded in plain text — the model can hallucinate fields, return the wrong type, omit required keys, or violate business constraints. Pydantic acts as a strict contract at that boundary, making agentic pipelines predictable and debuggable regardless of how many agents the pipeline contains.
Without structured output validation, every field access on LLM-produced data is a potential runtime crash. When an LLM returns
, the error surfaces far from its origin — possibly in a database write or a reporting step, not at the LLM call. Tracing that error back to the model response hours later is expensive.

The problem compounds in multi-step agentic pipelines. If Agent A extracts a document and passes a malformed object to Agent B, which passes something worse to Agent C, by the time Agent D fails you have no idea which step introduced the error. Pydantic validation at every agent boundary catches the error at the exact step that produced it, with a precise description: the field name, the received value, and what was expected. That specificity also enables self-healing retry loops — the error can be formatted and fed back to the LLM as additional context so it corrects itself in the next attempt.
ValidationError lists every failed field in one structured exception, with the bad input value included. No guessing about which field caused the crash.
A Pydantic model is simultaneously a Python class, a JSON Schema, and living documentation. Calling model.model_json_schema() produces a schema to embed directly in LLM prompts.
Pydantic v2 can coerce "42" to int 42 or "true" to bool True in lax mode, reducing spurious validation failures from LLMs that stringify numbers.
A field can itself be a Pydantic model, and validation is recursive. Complex hierarchical JSON structures are validated in full automatically.
Because fields are typed class attributes, editors offer autocomplete on the result object. result.title is known to be str; result.confidence is float.
model.model_dump() and model.model_dump_json() serialize validated objects back to JSON for storage or inter-service communication without extra effort.
Both the Anthropic and OpenAI APIs accept a JSON Schema generated from a Pydantic model to constrain generation at the token level, reducing JSON parse failures.
Every new output shape requires writing a Pydantic model. For exploratory work with rapidly changing schemas, this adds friction compared to raw dict access.
With extra='forbid', any unexpected field from the LLM fails validation. LLMs sometimes add helpful context fields not in your schema, and those get rejected.
Pydantic v2 broke backward compatibility. Legacy code using @validator, class Config, and dict() will emit deprecation warnings and can fail silently when mixed with v2.
In lax mode, Pydantic converts "3.7" to float 3.7. That is convenient but means the LLM can return the wrong type and validation will still pass.
Not a substitute for business logic.
Pydantic ensures shape and types are correct. It cannot verify a confidence score is accurate or that a cited URL actually exists. Domain checks still require application-level logic.
with a dictionary, Pydantic executes a fixed sequence of steps:
Field assignment: each key in the input dict is matched to a declared field by name (or alias).
Type coercion (lax mode) or strict type checking (strict mode): values are converted to the declared Python type or rejected.
Constraint checks: Field-level constraints (ge, le, min_length, max_length, pattern) are applied.
functions run with access to the fully populated model instance, enabling cross-field checks.
If all steps pass: a validated model instance is returned. If any step fails: all failures are collected and a single ValidationError is raised.
The validation core in Pydantic v2 is implemented in Rust (

). This means the hot path — type checking and coercion — runs at near-native speed, typically 5–50x faster than pure-Python Pydantic v1. For most agentic workloads, validation time is negligible compared to LLM inference latency.
produces a JSON Schema (Draft 7) that you can embed directly in your system prompt. This schema tells the LLM exactly which fields to include, which are optional, and what constraints apply.
, a list of error dicts. Each dict contains
(the actual value that failed). This structured format makes it straightforward to format the errors into a string and append them to the next LLM request as self-correction context.
The diagram below shows the full validation pipeline from LLM response to typed Python object, including the error feedback loop used for automatic retry:

attrs.alt: Pydantic Output Validation Pipeline — LLM output flows through JSON extraction, Pydantic model_validate(), and branches to a typed object on success or ValidationError with retry feedback on failure. Bottom panel shows the three model components: field declarations, custom validators, and model config.

content.text: The pipeline has four primary stages. First, the LLM produces a raw string containing JSON. Second,
converts the string to a Python dict, catching malformed JSON with
runs the full Pydantic validation pipeline. On success, a typed object is returned. On failure, the structured
is serialized to a readable string and appended to the next LLM request, creating a self-correcting retry loop.
Any agentic pipeline step that consumes LLM output as structured data.
Document information extraction where fields have types, constraints, and cross-field relationships.
Tool call response validation where a tool must return a typed result to the orchestrator.
Multi-agent handoffs where Agent A produces data that Agent B depends on.
Evaluation pipelines where LLM-as-judge must return numeric scores with defined ranges.
Pure streaming text output rendered directly to a user — Pydantic cannot validate a partially received stream.
Exploratory or prototype code where the schema is not yet stable — write the schema after you understand the output shape.
Ultra-low-latency paths where even the small overhead of model instantiation matters — use a hand-rolled dict check instead.
Output that is inherently free-form prose — forcing prose into a schema adds no value.
Declare each field with a Python type annotation. Use
for required fields with constraints. Use
text — it appears in the JSON Schema and guides the LLM.
Step 3: Call the LLM and Validate with Retry
Embed the machine-generated JSON Schema in your prompt. Extract JSON from the response (handling markdown code fences), then call
. Wrap this in a retry loop that passes

import json, re
import anthropic
from pydantic import ValidationError

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

_SCHEMA = json.dumps(ResearchFinding.model_json_schema(), indent=2)  # cache at module level

SYSTEM = f"""You are a research assistant. Extract findings from documents.
Return ONLY a JSON object matching this schema (no markdown, no explanation):
{_SCHEMA}"""

def extract_json(text: str) -> dict:
    match = re.search(r"```(?:json)?\s*(.+?)```", text, re.DOTALL)
    return json.loads(match.group(1) if match else text.strip())

def extract_finding(document: str, max_retries: int = 3) -> ResearchFinding:
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": f"Extract a finding from:\n\n{document}"}]
    last_error: str | None = None

    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        if last_error:
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": "<previous response>"})
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content":
                f"Validation failed:\n{last_error}\nReturn corrected JSON only."})

        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5", max_tokens=1024,
            system=SYSTEM, messages=messages,
        )
        raw = response.content[0].text
        messages[-1]["content"] = raw  # store actual response for next retry

        try:
            return ResearchFinding.model_validate(extract_json(raw))
        except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
            last_error = f"Invalid JSON: {e}"
        except ValidationError as e:
            errs = e.errors(include_url=False)
            lines = [
                f"  - {'.'.join(str(l) for l in err['loc'])}: {err['msg']} (got {err['input']!r})"
                for err in errs
            ]
            last_error = "Validation errors:\n" + "\n".join(lines)

    raise RuntimeError(f"Failed after {max_retries} attempts. Last: {last_error}")

from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal
import anthropic, json

class SentimentResult(BaseModel):
    label: Literal["positive", "negative", "neutral"]
    score: float  # 0.0 to 1.0
    reasoning: str

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def classify_sentiment(text: str) -> SentimentResult:
    schema = SentimentResult.model_json_schema()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": (
            f"Classify the sentiment. Respond with JSON matching: {json.dumps(schema)}\n\n"
            f"Text: {text}"
        )}]
    )
    return SentimentResult.model_validate(json.loads(response.content[0].text))

result = classify_sentiment("The new product launch exceeded all expectations.")
print(result.label)       # positive
print(result.score)       # e.g. 0.92
print(result.reasoning)

from __future__ import annotations
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, field_validator
from typing import Optional
import anthropic, json

class Citation(BaseModel):
    text: str = Field(..., min_length=5)
    page: Optional[int] = Field(None, ge=1)

class AnalysisSection(BaseModel):
    heading: str
    content: str = Field(..., min_length=20)
    citations: list[Citation] = Field(default_factory=list)

class DocumentAnalysis(BaseModel):
    document_id: str
    word_count: int = Field(..., ge=1)
    sections: list[AnalysisSection] = Field(..., min_length=1)
    overall_sentiment: float = Field(..., ge=-1.0, le=1.0)
    topics: list[str] = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=10)

    @field_validator("topics")
    @classmethod
    def topics_lowercase(cls, v: list[str]) -> list[str]:
        return [t.lower().strip() for t in v]

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
schema = DocumentAnalysis.model_json_schema()
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5", max_tokens=2048,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": (
        f"Analyze this document and respond with JSON matching:\n{json.dumps(schema, indent=2)}\n\n"
        "Document: [insert document text here]"
    )}]
)
analysis = DocumentAnalysis.model_validate(json.loads(response.content[0].text))
print(f"Found {len(analysis.sections)} sections, {len(analysis.topics)} topics")
Legal-Tech Pipeline: 50,000 Court Filings per Day

A legal-tech company processes court filings to extract structured case summaries. Each document is 5–150 pages and must be parsed into a typed model before being written to PostgreSQL and indexed for search. The pipeline runs on AWS Lambda with 200 concurrent workers and must maintain a >95% first-attempt validation pass rate.
At 50,000 documents per day, Pydantic validation adds under 2 ms per document (v2 with Rust core). The primary latency driver is the LLM call. The pipeline tracks a
metric — a rate below 90% triggers an alert indicating that either the prompt needs updating or the schema has drifted from the actual documents.
Mistake 1: Using Pydantic v1 Syntax in a v2 Project
Mistake 2: Catching bare Exception and Losing Structured Error Detail
, Pydantic silently drops any extra fields the LLM invents. This hides hallucinated keys that may indicate the LLM misunderstood the schema.

# WRONG — hallucinated field silently dropped
class Loose(BaseModel):
    title: str

m = Loose.model_validate({"title": "Test", "hallucinated_field": 99})
# m.hallucinated_field -> AttributeError (field was silently discarded)

# RIGHT — extra fields cause a ValidationError you can act on
class Strict(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
    title: str

Strict.model_validate({"title": "Test", "hallucinated_field": 99})
# -> ValidationError: Extra inputs are not permitted
involves Python-level reflection and is slower than validation. Generating it inside a hot request loop adds unnecessary overhead. Cache it at module level.

# WRONG — schema generated on every request
def extract(doc: str) -> ResearchFinding:
    schema = json.dumps(ResearchFinding.model_json_schema())  # slow!
    ...

# RIGHT — computed once at import time
_SCHEMA = json.dumps(ResearchFinding.model_json_schema(), indent=2)

def extract(doc: str) -> ResearchFinding:
    # use _SCHEMA in prompt
    ...
Extra fields are a signal of LLM confusion. Make them visible as ValidationError rather than silently discarding them.
Pass the auto-generated JSON Schema to the LLM. It is more precise than English descriptions and stays correct automatically when you change the model.
Field descriptions appear in the JSON Schema and help the LLM understand what each field should contain.
Use Literal types for categorical fields.
Literal['low', 'medium', 'high'] produces an enum in the JSON Schema, which is far more constraining than a bare str.
Prefer a hierarchy of small models over one large flat model. Small models are easier to test, easier to evolve, and produce shorter schemas.
Pass ValidationError details back to the LLM.
The error messages are human-readable. LLMs can usually fix validation errors in one retry when given the specific field name and error description.
When you add required fields, create a new versioned model (DocumentAnalysisV2) rather than mutating the original, to preserve backward compatibility.
Test @field_validator and @model_validator logic in isolation with controlled inputs — validators can have bugs too.
Pydantic v2 uses a compiled Rust core (
). Typical validation times for models with 10–20 fields are in the range of

per call. For a pipeline making 1,000 LLM calls per minute, the total validation overhead is under 2 seconds per minute — negligible compared to LLM inference times of 2–30 seconds per call. Pydantic validation releases the Python GIL for its Rust core, so it does not block concurrent LLM requests in a thread pool.
For batch pipelines processing thousands of documents, avoid accumulating model instances in memory. Process each document, serialize the result immediately with

or write it to storage, then let the instance be garbage collected. Track a validation pass rate metric in production: a rate below 90% indicates prompt drift or schema drift and should trigger a review before the failed retries cause excessive LLM spend.

patches the Anthropic and OpenAI clients to automatically extract, validate, and retry Pydantic model responses. It handles JSON extraction, retry logic, and error formatting out of the box, eliminating boilerplate.

import instructor
import anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel

class UserProfile(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int
    interests: list[str]

client = instructor.from_anthropic(anthropic.Anthropic())

profile = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=512,
    response_model=UserProfile,   # Pydantic model; instructor handles extraction + retry
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Extract profile: Jane, 28, loves hiking and painting"}]
)
print(profile.name)       # Jane
print(type(profile.age))  # <class 'int'>

is an agentic framework built directly on Pydantic. It uses Pydantic models for agent input/output schemas, tool parameter validation, and result types. The Anthropic API also accepts a JSON Schema derived from a Pydantic model in the
forcing structured output at the token level, which eliminates most JSON-parse failures and reduces the need for retry loops.

import anthropic, json
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Extraction(BaseModel):
    headline: str
    entities: list[str]
    sentiment: float

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
schema = Extraction.model_json_schema()

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5", max_tokens=512,
    tools=[{"name": "extract_data", "description": "Extract structured data",
             "input_schema": schema}],
    tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "extract_data"},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Extract: 'Apple stock surged 5% after earnings beat'"}],
)
result = Extraction.model_validate(response.content[0].input)
print(result.entities)   # ['Apple']
print(result.sentiment)  # e.g. 0.85
If LLM output is used to construct database queries, file paths, or shell commands before Pydantic validates it, a prompt injection attack can slip through. Always validate with Pydantic
using any field value in a downstream operation. Pydantic's constraint checks (pattern, min_length, max_length) also help sanitize inputs by rejecting payloads that exceed expected shapes.
by default. Use these for fields containing credentials or PII to prevent accidental logging.

from pydantic import BaseModel, SecretStr

class ApiCredentials(BaseModel):
    api_key: SecretStr
    endpoint: str

creds = ApiCredentials(api_key="sk-secret-123", endpoint="https://api.example.com")
print(creds)            # api_key=SecretStr('**********') endpoint='...'
print(creds.api_key.get_secret_value())  # sk-secret-123
Use a regex or the API's structured-output mode to extract JSON. Always catch json.JSONDecodeError separately from ValidationError.
ValidationError will list them. Pass the error back to the LLM. If it happens consistently, make the fields Optional with a default.
When you add required fields to a model, existing stored documents fail to deserialize. Version your schemas and maintain migration logic.
The JSON Schema embedded in prompts may be logged or visible in API response metadata. If it reveals sensitive field names (ssn, credit_card_number), consider your threat model.

In high-throughput pipelines, consistent validation failures cause every document to trigger 3 LLM calls. Add a circuit breaker that halts the batch and alerts when the failure rate exceeds a threshold.
Pydantic models are the standard boundary enforcement layer between LLM output and application code in agentic AI pipelines — use them at every agent handoff.
Always set extra='forbid' so hallucinated fields surface as explicit errors rather than silently disappearing.
Embed the auto-generated JSON Schema (model.model_json_schema()) in your LLM prompt so the model knows exactly what to produce — it is more reliable than informal English descriptions.
Pass the full ValidationError detail back to the LLM when retrying — the field name and error message are enough for the model to self-correct in most cases.
Pydantic v2 validation is negligibly fast (< 2 ms per call) compared to LLM inference; the performance cost of adding structured validation is effectively zero.
Use SecretStr for sensitive fields and add pattern/length constraints as a first line of defense against prompt injection payloads.
Tools like instructor eliminate the boilerplate of JSON extraction, schema embedding, and retry loops — prefer them over hand-rolling the same logic in production code.
Track a validation pass rate metric in production; a sustained drop below 90% indicates prompt drift or schema drift that needs immediate review.
